era_context: The late eighteenth century was defined by the convergence of Enlightenment thought and revolutionary upheaval. The American War of Independence, now in its fifth year, had drawn France (1778), Spain (1779), and soon the Dutch Republic into a global conflict against Britain that extended to the Caribbean, West Africa, and India. In South America, centuries of colonial exploitation were reaching a breaking point among indigenous and mestizo populations. In Europe, Enlightened Absolutism was transforming governance under rulers like Joseph II and Catherine the Great, while the Ottoman Empire under Abdülhamid I navigated Russian expansionism. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most populous state, and the Maratha Confederacy and Kingdom of Mysore contested British influence in India.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading and slave-trade records
Aboriginal Australian societies prior to sustained European contact
Pacific Island communities beyond initial European exploration accounts
Central Asian khanates between Qing and Russian spheres of influence
Interior Southeast Asian polities beyond court chronicles

events.title: Fall of Charleston to British forces
events.description: On 12 May 1780, after a six-week siege, Major General Benjamin Lincoln surrendered Charleston, South Carolina, to a British force under Sir Henry Clinton and Admiral Mariot Arbuthnot. Approximately 5,500 Continental soldiers and militia were captured along with four ships and a large store of weapons — the largest American surrender of the entire war. The fall of Charleston gave Britain control of the most important port in the southern colonies and launched the Southern Campaign that would define the war's final phase. Clinton returned to New York, leaving Lord Cornwallis to consolidate British control of the Carolinas.
key_figures: Sir Henry Clinton (British Commander-in-Chief, North America)
Benjamin Lincoln (Continental Army commander at Charleston)

sources.name: Clinton's dispatches to Lord George Germain, May–June 1780
Articles of Capitulation, Charleston, 12 May 1780
Carl P. Borick, A Gallant Defense: The Siege of Charleston, 1780 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in British and American military records, personal correspondence, and contemporary newspapers.

events.title: Battle of Camden — Destruction of Gates's army
events.description: On 16 August 1780, Lord Cornwallis routed an American army under Major General Horatio Gates at Camden, South Carolina. The American force, heavily reliant on untrained militia, collapsed under a British bayonet charge. Baron de Kalb, commanding the Continental regulars, was mortally wounded. American casualties exceeded 1,000 killed and wounded with roughly 1,000 captured, against fewer than 350 British casualties. The disaster destroyed Gates's reputation — he had fled the field on horseback — and led to his replacement by Nathanael Greene in December. Camden marked the nadir of American fortunes in the South.
key_figures: Baron Johann de Kalb (Continental division commander, killed)

sources.name: Cornwallis's after-action dispatch to Clinton, 21 August 1780
John Buchanan, The Road to Guilford Courthouse: The American Revolution in the Carolinas (1997)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in both British and American military records. Casualty figures are approximate but broadly agreed upon.

events.title: Battle of Kings Mountain — Patriot victory over Loyalists
events.region: South Carolina / North Carolina border, British North America
events.description: On 7 October 1780, approximately 900 Patriot frontier militia surrounded and destroyed a force of roughly 1,100 Loyalist militia under Major Patrick Ferguson at Kings Mountain, near the North Carolina–South Carolina border. Ferguson, the only British regular present, was killed. The Loyalists suffered over 300 killed, 160 wounded, and nearly 700 captured. The battle was fought entirely between Americans and marked a decisive turning point in the Southern Campaign, shattering Cornwallis's left flank and forcing him to postpone his planned invasion of North Carolina. Thomas Jefferson later called it 'the turn of the tide of success.'

sources.name: Lyman C. Draper, King's Mountain and Its Heroes (1881), drawing on participant accounts
Pension applications and depositions of Patriot veterans, collected early 19th century
Robert M. Dunkerly, The Battle of Kings Mountain: Eyewitness Accounts (2007)

events.certainty_note: Well-attested through participant memoirs and contemporary correspondence, though exact casualty figures vary slightly by source.

events.title: Treason of Benedict Arnold discovered
events.description: In late September 1780, the conspiracy of Major General Benedict Arnold to surrender the fortress at West Point to the British was uncovered when Arnold's British contact, Major John André, was captured carrying incriminating documents on 23 September. Arnold escaped to a British warship on the Hudson River on 25 September and was commissioned as a brigadier general in the British Army. André was tried as a spy by a military tribunal and hanged on 2 October. The Arnold affair shocked the American public and became the defining narrative of treason in American culture, but its military impact was limited since the plot was foiled before West Point could be surrendered.
key_figures: Peggy Shippen Arnold (Arnold's wife, suspected accomplice)

sources.name: Proceedings of a Board of General Officers Respecting Major John André, September 29, 1780
Washington's correspondence regarding Arnold, September–October 1780 (Papers of George Washington)
Nathaniel Philbrick, Valiant Ambition: George Washington, Benedict Arnold, and the Fate of the American Revolution (2016)

events.certainty_note: Among the most thoroughly documented episodes of the American Revolution, with extensive surviving correspondence from all parties.

events.title: Pennsylvania Gradual Abolition Act
events.description: On 1 March 1780, the Pennsylvania legislature passed 'An Act for the Gradual Abolition of Slavery,' the first abolition legislation enacted by any government in the Western Hemisphere. The act did not immediately free any enslaved person; instead, children born to enslaved mothers after the act's passage would be held as indentured servants until age 28. Existing enslaved people remained in bondage for life unless voluntarily manumitted. Though conservative and incrementalist by later standards, the law established a legislative precedent for emancipation and reflected Quaker abolitionist influence in Pennsylvania politics. It served as a model for similar gradual abolition laws in other northern states.
key_figures: George Bryan (acting president of Pennsylvania, key supporter)

sources.name: An Act for the Gradual Abolition of Slavery, Pennsylvania Statutes at Large, 1 March 1780
Gary B. Nash and Jean R. Soderlund, Freedom by Degrees: Emancipation in Pennsylvania and Its Aftermath (1991)

events.certainty_note: Legislative text survives in full. Date and provisions are not contested.

events.title: Gordon Riots in London
events.description: From 2 to 9 June 1780, London experienced the most destructive riots in its history. Triggered by Lord George Gordon's Protestant Association march to Parliament protesting the Catholic Relief Act of 1778, the unrest spiraled into widespread arson, looting, and attacks on Catholic chapels, prisons, and private homes. Newgate Prison and several other gaols were stormed and burned. The army was called in, and troops fired on crowds; approximately 285 rioters were killed, with 25 later executed. The riots exposed the fragility of public order in Georgian London and led to stronger policing measures, influencing the eventual creation of the Metropolitan Police in 1829.
key_figures: Lord George Gordon (instigator of the protests)
Lord Mansfield (Lord Chief Justice, whose house was attacked)

sources.name: Ignatius Sancho, Letters, describing the riots as an eyewitness (1782 publication)
Parliamentary debates and court records, June–July 1780
Ian Haywood and John Seed (eds.), The Gordon Riots: Politics, Culture, and Insurrection in Late Eighteenth-Century Britain (2012)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in newspapers, government records, personal letters, and court proceedings.

events.title: Formation of the League of Armed Neutrality
events.description: On 11 March 1780 (28 February OS), Empress Catherine the Great of Russia issued a declaration asserting the rights of neutral nations to trade freely with belligerent powers during wartime, specifically targeting British naval practices of searching and seizing neutral shipping. Denmark-Norway and Sweden joined Russia in a League of Armed Neutrality by July and August respectively; Prussia, the Holy Roman Empire, the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies, and Portugal eventually adhered. Though the League never engaged Britain militarily, it diplomatically isolated Britain, constrained the Royal Navy's ability to enforce its blockade of France and the American colonies, and established principles of neutral maritime rights that influenced international law for over a century.
key_figures: Catherine the Great (Empress of Russia, initiator)
Nikita Panin (Russian foreign minister, architect of the declaration)

sources.name: Declaration of Empress Catherine II on Armed Neutrality, 11 March 1780
Isabel de Madariaga, Britain, Russia, and the Armed Neutrality of 1780 (1962)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic texts survive; exact dates of adherence by secondary powers vary slightly by source but are well-attested.

events.title: Revolt of Túpac Amaru II begins in Peru
events.region: Viceroyalty of Peru, Spanish South America
events.description: On 4 November 1780, José Gabriel Condorcanqui, claiming descent from the last Inca emperor and styling himself Túpac Amaru II, launched a rebellion against Spanish colonial rule by capturing and publicly executing the Spanish corregidor Antonio de Arriaga near Tinta in the Cusco region. The uprising rapidly grew into the largest anti-colonial revolt in the history of Spanish America, eventually engulfing much of modern Peru and Bolivia and mobilizing tens of thousands of indigenous and mestizo supporters. Túpac Amaru's grievances centered on the brutal mita forced-labour system, excessive taxation, and abuses by colonial officials. The revolt would continue into 1781, when Túpac Amaru was captured and executed, but it profoundly shook Spanish imperial confidence and prompted significant administrative reforms.
key_figures: José Gabriel Condorcanqui / Túpac Amaru II (rebel leader)
Micaela Bastidas (Túpac Amaru's wife and key military organizer)

sources.name: Proclamations of Túpac Amaru II, November 1780 (Colección Documental de la Independencia del Perú)
Charles F. Walker, The Tupac Amaru Rebellion (2014)
Scarlett O'Phelan Godoy, Un siglo de rebeliones anticoloniales: Perú y Bolivia, 1700–1783 (1988)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Spanish colonial administrative records, trial proceedings, and rebel proclamations.

events.title: Great Hurricane of 1780 devastates the Caribbean
events.description: Between 10 and 16 October 1780, the deadliest Atlantic hurricane in recorded history struck the Lesser Antilles. Estimated fatalities reached 22,000 or more, with Barbados, Martinique, and Sint Eustatius suffering catastrophic destruction. On Barbados, nearly every building was leveled and trees were stripped of bark. The hurricane also devastated British and French naval forces operating in the Caribbean theater of the American Revolutionary War, sinking or damaging dozens of warships and transports on both sides. A Spanish fleet under Bernardo de Gálvez, preparing to attack Pensacola, was scattered. The storm was one of three major hurricanes to strike the Caribbean in October 1780, an unprecedented cluster.
key_figures: Bernardo de Gálvez (Spanish governor of Louisiana, fleet scattered)
Comte de Guichen (French naval commander, ships damaged)

sources.name: Governor James Cunninghame's reports from Barbados, October 1780
Stuart B. Schwartz, Sea of Storms: A History of Hurricanes in the Greater Caribbean (2015)
Naval dispatches from Rodney and de Guichen, October–November 1780

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in colonial governor reports, naval logs, and contemporary newspapers. Death toll estimates vary widely (20,000–27,500) due to incomplete records.

events.title: Death of Empress Maria Theresa of Austria
events.description: On 29 November 1780, Empress Maria Theresa, co-ruler of the Habsburg dominions since 1765 and effective sovereign since 1740, died in Vienna at age 63. Her death removed the last restraint on her son Joseph II, who had chafed under the co-regency and immediately launched a sweeping programme of Enlightened reforms including religious toleration, abolition of serfdom in parts of the Empire, and centralization of administration. Maria Theresa's 40-year reign had seen the loss of Silesia to Prussia, the Seven Years' War, the first partition of Poland, and significant internal modernization of the Habsburg state. Her death marked the transition from cautious reform to radical top-down transformation under Joseph II.
key_figures: Maria Theresa (Empress, Archduchess of Austria, Queen of Hungary and Bohemia)
Joseph II (son and successor as sole ruler)

sources.name: Court records and dispatches from Vienna, November–December 1780
Barbara Stollberg-Rilinger, Maria Theresa: The Habsburg Empress in Her Time (2021, English translation)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances are precisely documented in court records.

events.title: Second Anglo-Mysore War — Hyder Ali invades the Carnatic
events.region: Southern India, Kingdom of Mysore / British Madras
events.description: In July 1780, Hyder Ali, the Sultan of Mysore, launched a devastating invasion of the Carnatic (the British-allied coastal region around Madras) with an army estimated at 80,000–90,000 troops, allied with the Marathas and the Nizam of Hyderabad. On 10 September 1780, Hyder Ali's forces destroyed a British detachment under Colonel William Baillie at the Battle of Pollilur, one of the worst British military disasters in India. The defeat shattered British prestige in southern India and forced the East India Company to divert resources from other theaters. The war reflected Indian rulers' strategic exploitation of Britain's global overextension during the American War.
key_figures: Tipu Sultan (Hyder Ali's son, field commander)
Colonel William Baillie (British commander, captured at Pollilur)
Sir Hector Munro (British commander, failed to relieve Baillie)

sources.name: East India Company records and dispatches from Madras Presidency, 1780
Mural depicting the Battle of Pollilur at Tipu Sultan's summer palace, Seringapatam
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy: Islam and Kingship in a Hindu Domain (1997)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in British East India Company records, Indian court chronicles, and French observers' accounts.

events.title: Joseph II issues Serfdom Patent for Bohemia
events.description: On 1 November 1780, shortly before Maria Theresa's death, Joseph II issued the Leibeigenschaftspatent (Serfdom Patent) abolishing personal serfdom (Leibeigenschaft) in Bohemia and its incorporated lands. Peasants gained the right to marry without their lord's permission, to move freely, and to learn trades. This was the first of Joseph's major reform edicts and the most far-reaching emancipation of serfs in 18th-century Europe prior to the French Revolution. However, labor obligations (Robot) were only modestly reduced, and noble landowners retained considerable economic power over the peasantry.
key_figures: Joseph II (Holy Roman Emperor and Habsburg ruler)

sources.name: Leibeigenschaftspatent, 1 November 1780 (published in Handbuch aller unter der Regierung des Kaisers Joseph des II. ergangenen Verordnungen)
T.C.W. Blanning, Joseph II (1994)

events.certainty_note: Legislative text survives. The exact scope of the patent is occasionally debated, but its issuance and core provisions are not contested.

events.title: Battle of Cape St. Vincent (Moonlight Battle)
events.region: Atlantic Ocean, off Cape St. Vincent, Portugal
events.description: On 16 January 1780, a British squadron under Admiral Sir George Rodney, escorting a supply convoy to Gibraltar, intercepted a Spanish squadron under Don Juan de Lángara off Cape St. Vincent. In an unusual night engagement — the 'Moonlight Battle' — Rodney captured or destroyed seven Spanish ships of the line. The victory temporarily relieved the siege of Gibraltar, demonstrated British naval competence despite the pressures of a multi-front war, and established Rodney's reputation as an aggressive fleet commander. Rodney subsequently proceeded to the Caribbean, where he would engage the French fleet.
key_figures: Don Juan de Lángara (Spanish commander, captured)

sources.name: Rodney's dispatches to the Admiralty, January 1780
N.A.M. Rodger, The Command of the Ocean: A Naval History of Britain, 1649–1815 (2004)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in British and Spanish naval records.

events.title: Dark Day in New England
events.description: On 19 May 1780, an extraordinary darkness descended over New England and parts of eastern Canada during midday, causing widespread alarm. Candles were required indoors, animals behaved as if it were night, and many people feared divine judgment. The event is now attributed to a combination of dense forest fire smoke from large fires burning in Ontario and upstate New York, heavy fog, and thick cloud cover. The Connecticut legislature famously continued its session by candlelight when Colonel Abraham Davenport refused to adjourn, reportedly saying, 'The Day of Judgment is either approaching or it is not.' The event left a lasting imprint on New England folklore and religious discourse.

sources.name: Samuel Williams, An Account of a Very Uncommon Darkness, Memoirs of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences (1783)
Erin R. McMurry et al., 'Fire scars reveal source of New England's 1780 Dark Day,' International Journal of Wildland Fire, 16 (2007)

events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in diaries, newspapers, and official records. The wildfire-smoke explanation is supported by modern dendrochronological evidence.

events.title: Watt and Boulton begin selling rotary steam engines
events.description: By 1780, the partnership of James Watt and Matthew Boulton at the Soho Manufactory in Birmingham was actively marketing improved steam engines with the separate condenser and, crucially, developing the sun-and-planet gear mechanism to convert reciprocating motion to rotary motion (patented 1781). While the fully rotary engine was not commercially available until 1782–83, 1780 marks the period when Boulton & Watt engines were being installed in increasing numbers in mines and manufactories across Britain, driving the early Industrial Revolution. By this year, over 40 engines had been erected, primarily for pumping in Cornish tin and copper mines.

sources.name: Boulton & Watt engine order books and correspondence, Soho Archives (Birmingham City Archives)
Jenny Uglow, The Lunar Men: Five Friends Whose Curiosity Changed the World (2002)

events.certainty_note: Engine installations and business records are well-documented. The transition to rotary motion was a process spanning 1780–1783 rather than a single event.

events.title: Founding of the Académie des sciences, belles-lettres et arts de Lyon reorganized
events.description: In 1780, Antoine Lavoisier published his experiments on the role of oxygen in combustion and respiration in collaboration with Pierre-Simon Laplace. Their landmark experiment used a guinea pig in an ice calorimeter to demonstrate that respiration is a slow form of combustion, measuring the heat produced by a living animal and comparing it to that released by burning charcoal. This work, presented to the Académie royale des sciences, was foundational to modern chemistry and physiology, overturning the phlogiston theory. Lavoisier's systematic quantitative approach exemplified the Chemical Revolution that was transforming natural philosophy.

sources.name: Lavoisier and Laplace, 'Mémoire sur la chaleur,' Mémoires de l'Académie royale des sciences (1780, read 1783)
Frederic L. Holmes, Lavoisier and the Chemistry of Life (1985)

events.certainty_note: The experiments were conducted in the winter of 1780–1781; the memoir was read to the Academy in 1783. Dating the experimental work itself to 1780 is well-supported by Lavoisier's laboratory notebooks.

events.title: Rebellion of Comuneros begins in New Granada
events.description: In 1780, growing fiscal pressure from Bourbon tax reforms — particularly new levies on tobacco, aguardiente, and the extension of the alcabala sales tax — provoked widespread unrest in the Viceroyalty of New Granada. While the full-scale Comunero Revolt erupted in March 1781 in Socorro, the groundwork was laid throughout 1780 with escalating protests and petitions against the reforms of Regent-Visitor Juan Francisco Gutiérrez de Piñeres. The unrest reflected broader patterns of colonial resistance across Spanish America, parallel to the Túpac Amaru uprising in Peru.
key_figures: Juan Francisco Gutiérrez de Piñeres (Spanish regent-visitor, imposed tax reforms)
Manuel del Socorro Rodríguez (chronicler of the period)

sources.name: John Leddy Phelan, The People and the King: The Comunero Revolution in Colombia, 1781 (1978)
Colonial administrative correspondence, Archivo General de la Nación, Bogotá

events.certainty_note: The fiscal reforms of 1780 are documented in colonial records. The full revolt is conventionally dated to 1781, but the causal buildup in 1780 is well-established.

events.title: Fourth Anglo-Dutch War precipitated by Dutch accession to Armed Neutrality
events.description: Throughout 1780, tensions between Britain and the Dutch Republic escalated over Dutch trade with France and the American rebels, and Dutch consideration of joining the League of Armed Neutrality. In November 1780, Britain pre-emptively declared war on the Dutch Republic before they could formally accede to the League (which would have extended the League's collective protection). The Fourth Anglo-Dutch War (1780–1784) proved catastrophic for the Dutch, devastating their overseas trade, weakening the VOC (Dutch East India Company), and triggering the Patriot political crisis of the 1780s that would reshape Dutch politics.
key_figures: John Adams (American envoy to the Netherlands)

sources.name: British declaration of war, December 1780 (London Gazette)
Jonathan Israel, The Dutch Republic: Its Rise, Greatness, and Fall, 1477–1806 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic records from both sides are extensive. The formal declaration was in December 1780, with hostilities beginning shortly after.

events.title: Tây Sơn rebellion advances in Vietnam
events.description: By 1780, the Tây Sơn brothers — Nguyễn Nhạc, Nguyễn Huệ, and Nguyễn Lữ — had consolidated control over much of central and southern Vietnam, having overthrown the Nguyễn lords in the south. The Tây Sơn movement, which had begun as a peasant uprising in 1771, was evolving into a formidable military-political force. In 1780, the Tây Sơn continued to consolidate power in the Mekong Delta region and clashed with Siamese-backed forces supporting the dispossessed Nguyễn Ánh. The rebellion would eventually unify Vietnam briefly before Nguyễn Ánh's ultimate victory in 1802.
key_figures: Nguyễn Huệ (military leader, future Emperor Quang Trung)
Nguyễn Ánh (dispossessed Nguyễn lord, future Emperor Gia Long)

sources.name: Đại Việt sử ký toàn thư (Complete Annals of Đại Việt) and later Nguyễn dynasty chronicles
George Dutton, The Tây Sơn Uprising: Society and Rebellion in Eighteenth-Century Vietnam (2006)

events.certainty_note: Vietnamese sources for this period are primarily from the victorious Nguyễn dynasty, which had reason to disparage the Tây Sơn. Exact military chronology for 1780 is less precise than for the rebellion's beginning and end.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaigns against frontier unrest
events.description: By 1780, the Qianlong Emperor was in the 45th year of his reign over the Qing Empire, which had reached its greatest territorial extent. In this year, a significant event was the discovery and suppression of a conspiracy by the Muslim leader Su Sishisan in Gansu Province, which revealed widespread corruption among Qing officials who had fabricated reports of conversions and embezzled relief funds. The case resulted in the execution of the provincial governor and numerous officials. The Gansu corruption scandal highlighted the administrative challenges facing the mature Qing state and the growing difficulties of governing a vast, diverse empire.
key_figures: Qianlong Emperor (Aisin Gioro Hongli, Qing ruler)
Wang Danwang (governor of Gansu, executed for corruption)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), Qianlong reign section
William T. Rowe, China's Last Empire: The Great Qing (2009)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Qing imperial records. The Gansu corruption case is a major episode in studies of Qing governance.

events.title: Paine publishes The American Crisis No. IX
events.description: Throughout 1780, Thomas Paine continued publishing installments of The American Crisis, a series of pamphlets intended to sustain American morale during the war's darkest period. Crisis No. IX, published in June 1780, addressed the dire military and financial situation following the fall of Charleston and argued for continued resistance and for adequate funding of the Continental Army. Paine also served as clerk of the Pennsylvania Assembly and was involved in founding the Bank of Pennsylvania (later Bank of North America) to raise funds for the army, blending his propagandist role with practical financial organization.
key_figures: Robert Morris (financier, collaborated on bank scheme)

sources.name: Thomas Paine, The American Crisis, No. IX (1780)
Eric Foner, Tom Paine and Revolutionary America (1976, revised 2005)

events.certainty_note: Published text survives. Paine's activities in 1780 are well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly held narratives about 1780 require qualification. First, the Battle of Kings Mountain is often presented as a purely spontaneous uprising of 'Overmountain Men,' but recent scholarship (e.g., Dunkerly 2007) shows significant coordination among militia leaders and prior intelligence about Ferguson's movements. Second, the Pennsylvania Gradual Abolition Act is frequently cited as the 'first abolition law,' which is accurate for legislative acts but overlooks that the 1777 Vermont Constitution prohibited adult slavery (though Vermont was not yet a recognized state). Third, the death toll of the Great Hurricane of 1780 is routinely cited as 22,000, but this figure derives from imprecise colonial estimates and may significantly undercount enslaved persons who died, as plantation records were often destroyed in the storm itself. Fourth, Benedict Arnold's treason is sometimes attributed primarily to personal grievance, but recent work (e.g., Philbrick 2016) emphasizes the role of his wife Peggy Shippen and her Loyalist social network as significant catalysts, complicating the individual-betrayal narrative. Fifth, the Lavoisier-Laplace ice calorimeter experiments are sometimes dated to 1783 (when the memoir was formally read); the experiments themselves were conducted in the winter of 1780–1781, and precise dating within that range is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The year 1780 is richly documented from European and Euro-American perspectives owing to the extensive records generated by the American Revolutionary War, European state archives, and Enlightenment-era scientific publishing. The Túpac Amaru rebellion is documented primarily through Spanish colonial judicial and administrative records, which carry inherent biases against the rebels. South and Southeast Asian events are filtered through colonial (British East India Company) records and later dynastic chronicles, both of which serve particular political narratives. Sub-Saharan Africa beyond the coastal slave trade remains largely invisible in the written record for this year. The Qing records are extensive but court-centered, potentially obscuring rural conditions. Overall, 1780 offers unusually global documentation because the American War had made it a genuinely worldwide conflict, drawing naval and military records from the Caribbean, Indian Ocean, Mediterranean, and Atlantic into a single interconnected narrative.

graph_edges.note: Fall of Charleston launched the Southern Campaign that culminated at Yorktown
Camden disaster led to Greene's appointment and the campaign culminating at Guilford Courthouse
Kings Mountain forced Cornwallis to delay his North Carolina invasion, contributing to the chain of events leading to Yorktown
Britain declared war on the Dutch partly to prevent them joining the Armed Neutrality with its protections
Túpac Amaru's November 1780 uprising escalated into 1781 before his capture and execution
Maria Theresa's death freed Joseph II to accelerate the reform program already begun with the Serfdom Patent
The Great Hurricane disrupted both British and French naval operations in the Caribbean theater of the same war
Both the Pennsylvania abolition act and Túpac Amaru's revolt reflected Enlightenment-era challenges to inherited social hierarchies, though arising from very different contexts
Hyder Ali's invasion of the Carnatic was strategically connected to Britain's overextension in the American War
The Comunero unrest in New Granada paralleled the Túpac Amaru revolt — both driven by Bourbon fiscal reforms
